title: Interesting Linux
topic: lesser-known but fascinating Linux kernel features and syscalls

concepts.title: Everything Is a File Descriptor (eventfd, timerfd, signalfd, pidfd)
Safer Ways to Open a File (O_PATH, O_TMPFILE, O_NOFOLLOW)
Zero-Copy Data Movement (splice, vmsplice, sendfile, tee)
Anonymous Memory Files (memfd_create and file sealing)
Epoll's Sharp Edges (edge- vs level-triggered, EPOLLONESHOT)
io_uring: Async I/O Without the Syscalls
Namespaces: Your Own Private Linux (PID and mount namespaces)

notes:
Audience is average Linux users and software developers. Add illustrations, funny notes, and zine-like image-slot prompts the reader can fill in. Delight the reader; explain each concept simply and suggest real-world use cases. Requested look: light themed, minimal but elegant (resolved to the 'codex' theme — warm paper, light background, elegant serif). Full seed brief from the requester, for research context:

This book is about interesting Linux features that are often not popular. This book should explain it and suggest usecases where this can be used. Each concept should be explained in a simple manner.

File descriptor-based abstractions ("everything is a fd"): eventfd (counter as a fd, used in KVM/virtio/event loops), timerfd (timers as fds for epoll loops), signalfd (signals delivered via a readable fd instead of async handlers), pidfd (a fd referring to a process, usable with pidfd_send_signal to avoid PID-reuse races).

File opening and path tricks: O_PATH (path-only fd, useful with fstat/fchown/readlink/openat, avoids TOCTOU bugs), O_TMPFILE (unnamed inode with no directory entry until linkat, avoiding partial-file visibility races; combine with O_EXCL), O_NOFOLLOW/AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW (refuse to follow symlinks on open, security-critical).

Zero-copy and efficient data movement: splice()/vmsplice() (move data between fds via the kernel page cache with no userspace copy — proxies, load balancers), sendfile() (zero-copy file-to-socket, classic web server technique), tee() (duplicate data between pipes without a userspace copy, for fan-out streaming).

Anonymous and in-memory files: memfd_create() (anonymous RAM-backed tmpfs file, mmap-able and shareable across processes via fd passing; used by containers, dynamic linkers, JITs), file sealing via F_ADD_SEALS/F_GET_SEALS (F_SEAL_WRITE, F_SEAL_SHRINK, etc. to make a memfd immutable for safe cross-process sharing).

Epoll subtleties: edge-triggered (EPOLLET) vs level-triggered — edge mode only notifies on state change so you must drain to EAGAIN, level mode notifies as long as ready; EPOLLONESHOT disables a fd after one event until re-armed with EPOLL_CTL_MOD, useful for thread-pool designs.

The new async I/O model: io_uring — ring-buffer-based async I/O, submission queue entries (SQE) and completion queue entries (CQE) shared via memory rings, drastically cutting syscall overhead; SQPOLL lets the kernel poll the ring so the hot path needs zero syscalls. Adopted by Postgres and other high-performance servers/databases.

Process and namespace curiosities: PID namespaces (a process sees a different PID set inside a namespace, enabling container isolation; pairs with pidfd to avoid global-PID assumptions), mount namespaces and bind mounts (each process can have its own mount tree / isolated filesystem view without chroot; underpins container runtimes).

Kernel tuning "secrets": vm.swappiness / vm.vfs_cache_pressure (control swap aggressiveness and inode/dentry cache eviction; defaults are often wrong for databases or low-latency workloads), Transparent Huge Pages (THP) — always/madvise/never modes affecting latency-sensitive apps, Pressure Stall Information (PSI) — real-time CPU/memory/IO contention telemetry, better than top for diagnosing resource pressure.

Good primary sources to check: man7.org man pages for each syscall (eventfd(2), timerfd_create(2), signalfd(2), pidfd_open(2), open(2) for O_PATH/O_TMPFILE/O_NOFOLLOW, splice(2), sendfile(2), tee(2), memfd_create(2), fcntl(2) for sealing, epoll(7), io_uring docs/liburing, pid_namespaces(7), mount_namespaces(7)), and LWN.net articles on O_TMPFILE, io_uring, and fd-ification (eventfd/timerfd/signalfd).
